page.text:
In some cases a choice function is easy to name. For instance, if $X$ is the
collection of all nonempty open intervals $(a, b)$ of real numbers, the map
$(a, b) \mapsto \frac{a + b}{2}$ sending each interval to its midpoint is a choice
function. In other cases there is no obvious choice function: if $X$ is the
collection of all nonempty subsets of the reals, there is no evident rule
assigning to each subset one of its elements, and the inductive construction used
for finite collections fails because $X$ is infinite. To guarantee a choice
function in general, one must take its existence as an axiom.

assigning to each subset one of its elements, and the inductive construction used
for finite collections fails because $X$ is infinite. To guarantee a choice
function in general, one must take its existence as an axiom.

"
In some cases a choice function is easy to name. For instance, if $X$ is the
collection of all nonempty open intervals $(a, b)$ of real numbers, the map
$(a, b) \mapsto \frac{a + b}{2}$ sending each interval to its midpoint is a choice
function. In other cases there is no obvious choice function: if $X$ is the
collection of all nonempty subsets of the reals, there is no evident rule
assigning to each subset one of its elements, and the inductive construction used
for finite collections fails because $X$ is infinite. To guarantee a choice
function in general, one must take its existence as an axiom.
"
